text: The surgeon begins the procedure by moving medially towards the pubic tubercle, initiating the dissection in the typical starting location for this type of hernia repair.
Scar tissue is visible anterior to the bladder, likely due to previous surgical procedures.
The surgeon begins dissecting the hernia sac emerging from the indirect inguinal space, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon continues dissection medially towards the pubic tubercle, navigating through scarred tissue planes due to the patient's previous robotic prostatectomy.
The surgeon encounters sticky tissue during the medial dissection, making it challenging to locate the pubic bone.
The surgeon continues dissection by moving laterally, carefully navigating the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully navigates the surgical field to locate the cord structures, ensuring they are not injured during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon creates several peritoneal holes during the reduction of the hernia sac, which are later patched using the hernia sac itself to cover the defects.
A small lipoma is visible as it is brought down during the dissection process.
The surgeon sets aside a structure temporarily and continues dissecting the hernia sac, focusing on separating it from surrounding tissues.
A visible hole is present in the peritoneum during the hernia sac dissection.
The surgeon begins to visualize the edge of the hernia sac during the dissection process.
The surgeon identifies the edge of the hernia sac and sweeps the chord structures down and away from the surgical field.
The surgeon continues to parietalize the cord structures, carefully separating them from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon identifies the pubic tubercle, a bony landmark, during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon continues to parietalize the vas, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues in the surgical field.
The surgeon creates an additional opening in the peritoneum at the specified location, revealing underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon encounters a section of tissue that is firmly adhered, requiring careful manipulation to separate it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon is unable to visualize the femoral vessels, which are obscured by a layer of adipose tissue.
The surgeon continues to dissect the area around the pubic tubercle to ensure proper placement of the mesh, aiming to reduce the risk of hernia recurrence.
The surgeon returns to work on the lipoma, attempting to dissect it further to achieve better positioning.
The surgeon amputates the lipoma, visibly severing its blood supply, which is typically preserved.
The surgeon performs further parietalization of the cord structures, manipulating them to achieve better positioning within the surgical field.
The surgeon attempts to locate a dissection plane over the femoral region, navigating through the surrounding adipose tissue.
The surgeon identifies a hemo lock clip within the surgical field.
The surgeon clears obstructive tissue from the surgical field, creating sufficient space for the mesh to be laid flat, which is intended to decrease the risk of hernia recurrence.
The surgeon places the mesh into the prepared pocket and carefully unfolds it in a sequential manner.
The surgeon proceeds to close the flap over the area where the mesh has been placed, ensuring it is securely positioned.
The surgeon manipulates loose redundant tissue to create a flap patch over the visible defects in the surgical field.
